source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Ito-Laplacian martingale for Brownian motion
summary: If v is C2 and the stochastic integral is square-integrable, then v(B_t)-1/2 integral_0^t Delta v(B_s) ds is a continuous martingale.
proof_idea: Apply Ito's formula to v(B_t); the drift is exactly 1/2 Delta v and the gradient term is a martingale.
exam_use: The one-line engine behind harmonic functions, optional stopping, PDE uniqueness, and Green function computations.
pitfalls: The integrability/localization condition is not decoration; without it the stochastic integral may only be local.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Mean exit time from a ball
summary: For d-dimensional Brownian motion started at |x|<R, the hitting time S_R of the sphere of radius R has E_x S_R=(R^2-|x|^2)/d.
proof_idea: Stop the martingale |B_t|^2-dt at S_R wedge t and let t go to infinity.
exam_use: Use for expected lifetime in balls and as a quick check for Poisson-equation solutions.
pitfalls: The dimension appears in the denominator because |B_t|^2-dt is summed over d coordinates.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Radial harmonic test functions
summary: The radial functions phi(x)=log|x| in d=2 and phi(x)=|x|^(2-d) in d>=3 are harmonic off the origin.
proof_idea: Differentiate radial functions or use the radial Laplacian formula g''(r)+(d-1)g'(r)/r.
exam_use: They convert annulus hitting probabilities into algebra.
pitfalls: They are singular at zero, so stop before hitting the inner radius and localize with smooth compact modifications.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Annulus hitting probability
summary: For r<|x|<R, P_x(S_r<S_R)=[phi(R)-phi(x)]/[phi(R)-phi(r)], with phi radial harmonic as above.
proof_idea: Optional stopping gives phi(x)=phi(r)P_x(S_r<S_R)+phi(R)P_x(S_R<S_r); solve the linear equation.
exam_use: This is the standard computation for recurrence in d=2 and transience in d>=3.
pitfalls: Use the correct dimension-specific phi; the logarithmic d=2 case is a frequent source of wrong powers.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Two-dimensional Brownian recurrence
summary: In d=2, Brownian motion hits every circle around the origin almost surely and visits every nonempty open set infinitely often.
proof_idea: In the annulus formula, fix r and let R go to infinity; the logarithmic ratio tends to one.
exam_use: Use to decide whether planar Brownian motion returns to neighborhoods, not to exact points.
pitfalls: Recurrence of neighborhoods does not mean hitting a prescribed point at positive time.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Points are polar in dimensions at least two
summary: For d>=2, Brownian motion does not hit a fixed point at a positive time, even when started from that point.
proof_idea: In d=2, let the inner radius shrink to zero in the annulus formula; in higher dimensions reduce or compare by projection.
exam_use: Prevents confusing recurrence in d=2 with point recurrence; important for punctured-domain examples.
pitfalls: The definition of hitting zero uses t>0, so starting at zero is not a counterexample.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Transience in dimensions at least three
summary: For d>=3, P_x(S_r<infinity)=(r/|x|)^(d-2) when |x|>r, and |B_t| tends to infinity almost surely.
proof_idea: Let the outer radius go to infinity in the annulus formula, then use the strong Markov property over growing spheres.
exam_use: Use whenever a problem asks whether Brownian motion returns to bounded sets in high dimension.
pitfalls: The limit |B_t| to infinity is almost sure but does not imply monotone radial motion.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Dvoretsky-Erdos lower envelope test
summary: For d>=3 and positive decreasing g, P_0(|B_t|<=g(t)sqrt(t) infinitely often) is one or zero according as integral g(t)^(d-2) dt/t diverges or converges.
proof_idea: This sharpens transience by testing repeated returns to shrinking multiples of sqrt(t).
exam_use: Useful as a recognition result for Brownian escape rates in advanced prelim-style questions.
pitfalls: It concerns infinitely often behavior near infinity, not fixed-time Gaussian tails.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Backward heat-equation martingale
summary: If u_t=1/2 Delta u, then M_s=u(t-s,B_s) is a local martingale on 0<=s<=t.
proof_idea: Apply Ito's formula to the space-time process (t-s,B_s); the time derivative cancels the Brownian drift.
exam_use: This is the probabilistic uniqueness mechanism for the heat equation.
pitfalls: The backward time t-s is essential; using t+s changes the sign.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Heat equation solution by Brownian expectation
summary: The bounded solution with initial condition f is v(t,x)=E_x f(B_t), equivalently convolution with the Gaussian heat kernel.
proof_idea: Boundedness makes the backward martingale uniformly integrable, so u(t,x)=E_x u(0,B_t).
exam_use: Use to solve Cauchy heat problems and identify semigroup smoothing.
pitfalls: Continuity at t=0 requires continuity of f; boundedness alone is not enough for the boundary condition.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Gaussian heat kernel smoothing
summary: For bounded continuous f, v(t,x)=integral (2 pi t)^(-d/2) exp(-|x-y|^2/(2t)) f(y) dy is C1,2 for t>0.
proof_idea: Differentiate the Gaussian kernel under the integral for positive times.
exam_use: Turns probabilistic representation into an actual classical solution.
pitfalls: The smoothing is for t>0; behavior at t=0 is controlled by the initial data.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Duhamel representation for inhomogeneous heat equation
summary: For u_t=1/2 Delta u+g with zero initial data, the bounded solution is v(t,x)=E_x integral_0^t g(B_s) ds.
proof_idea: Ito's formula shows u(t-s,B_s)+integral_0^s g(B_r)dr is a local martingale; stop at s=t.
exam_use: Use for heat equations with source terms and for occupation-time interpretations.
pitfalls: With nonzero f, add the homogeneous solution E_x f(B_t).

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Holder source gives C1,2 inhomogeneous solution
summary: If g is Holder continuous, then v(t,x)=E_x integral_0^t g(B_s) ds is C1,2 and solves the inhomogeneous heat equation.
proof_idea: Write v as integration of heat kernels against g and use parabolic regularity estimates.
exam_use: A checklist item: representation plus Holder regularity gives a classical PDE solution.
pitfalls: Continuity of g is natural, but Holder regularity is the clean sufficient condition used here.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Feynman-Kac formula for the heat equation with potential
summary: For u_t=1/2 Delta u+c(x)u and initial f, the bounded solution is v(t,x)=E_x[f(B_t) exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds)].
proof_idea: Use Ito plus the product rule on u(t-s,B_s) exp(integral_0^s c(B_r)dr); the drift cancels.
exam_use: Use for heat equations with growth/decay potential and moment-generating questions for occupation integrals.
pitfalls: Check the sign convention: the chapter's plus c u corresponds to exp(+ integral c).

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Feynman-Kac smoothness via reduction
summary: If f and c are Holder continuous, the Feynman-Kac representation is C1,2 and solves the PDE.
proof_idea: Split the representation into a heat part and an inhomogeneous heat part with source c(x)v(t,x).
exam_use: Use when asked to justify that a probabilistic formula is a classical solution.
pitfalls: Boundedness gives a representation; regularity needs additional assumptions.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Dirichlet problem representation
summary: For a domain G with exit time tau, any bounded harmonic solution with boundary data f must be v(x)=E_x f(B_tau).
proof_idea: Run u(B_t) until exit, use bounded martingale convergence, and identify the terminal boundary value.
exam_use: The central Brownian representation for harmonic measure and boundary-value problems.
pitfalls: The formula can fail to satisfy the boundary condition at irregular boundary points.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Mean-value property implies harmonicity
summary: If v(x)=E_x v(B_tau_B) for every small ball B around x and v is C2, then Delta v(x)=0.
proof_idea: Use Taylor expansion at x and the symmetry of the exit distribution from a ball.
exam_use: Use to move from Brownian representations to Laplace's equation without stochastic calculus.
pitfalls: The ball must be compactly contained in the domain.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Interior smoothness of Brownian harmonic functions
summary: The Brownian Dirichlet solution v(x)=E_x f(B_tau) is C-infinity inside G.
proof_idea: Inside a ball, v equals an average over the sphere with the smooth Poisson kernel, so differentiation is allowed.
exam_use: Lets you assert interior classical harmonicity even with only bounded boundary data.
pitfalls: Interior smoothness says nothing by itself about boundary continuity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Regular boundary point
summary: A boundary point y is regular for G if Brownian motion started at y exits G immediately: P_y(tau=0)=1.
proof_idea: Regularity is characterized probabilistically by immediate contact with the complement.
exam_use: Use to decide where the Brownian Dirichlet solution attains the prescribed boundary value.
pitfalls: A boundary point can be topologically present but probabilistically invisible.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Boundary continuity at regular points
summary: If f is bounded continuous and y is a regular boundary point, then E_x f(B_tau) tends to f(y) as x in G tends to y.
proof_idea: Lower semicontinuity of x -> P_x(tau<=t) plus Brownian path continuity forces exits near y with high probability.
exam_use: This is the precise condition for the Dirichlet representation to satisfy the boundary data.
pitfalls: Do not claim boundary convergence at every boundary point without checking regularity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Cone condition for regularity
summary: If the complement of G contains a cone with vertex y inside some small ball, then y is a regular boundary point.
proof_idea: Brownian motion hits such exterior cones immediately enough to force tau=0 from the boundary.
exam_use: A practical geometric sufficient condition for boundary regularity.
pitfalls: It is sufficient, not necessary; failure of a cone condition does not automatically mean irregularity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Punctured-domain boundary warning
summary: Thin removed sets can fail to influence Brownian exit, so a bounded harmonic representation may ignore assigned values on those sets.
proof_idea: Use polarity of points or lines in higher dimensions to see that Brownian motion almost surely misses the thin set.
exam_use: Explains why Dirichlet boundary data must be paired with regularity assumptions.
pitfalls: A discontinuity of the probabilistic solution at an irregular point is not a contradiction of uniqueness inside G.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Half-space Poisson kernel
summary: For the upper half-space H, the exit distribution on the boundary has density C_d y/(|x-theta|^2+y^2)^(d/2).
proof_idea: Check the kernel is harmonic in H, normalizes to one, and approximates boundary data as y down to zero.
exam_use: Use to compute Brownian exit distributions from a half-space; in d=2 it gives the Cauchy density.
pitfalls: Track dimension conventions: the boundary variable theta lives in d-1 dimensions.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Unit-ball Poisson kernel
summary: For the unit ball D, E_x f(B_tau)=integral_{partial D} [(1-|x|^2)/|x-y|^d] f(y) pi(dy).
proof_idea: Show the kernel is harmonic in x and reproduces the boundary data as x approaches regular boundary points.
exam_use: Use to solve explicit Dirichlet problems in balls.
pitfalls: The surface measure pi is normalized to be a probability measure in the chapter's formula.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Brownian potential kernel in R^d
summary: The expected occupation density of Brownian motion is the potential kernel G(x,y), proportional to |x-y|^(2-d) for d>=3 and to -log|x-y| in d=2 after domain adjustment.
proof_idea: Integrate the Gaussian transition density over time and evaluate the resulting radial integral.
exam_use: Use to turn occupation-time expectations into spatial integrals.
pitfalls: In recurrent dimensions the full-space occupation integral can diverge, so constants and killed domains matter.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Killed-domain Green function
summary: For Brownian motion killed on leaving D, G_D(x,y) is the density satisfying E_x integral_0^tau f(B_t)dt = integral_D G_D(x,y)f(y)dy.
proof_idea: Subtract the expected potential contributed after exit from the full-space potential.
exam_use: The bridge between Brownian occupation times and Poisson's equation.
pitfalls: The Green function depends on the domain and boundary killing, not only on |x-y|.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Poisson equation representation
summary: For 1/2 Delta u=-g in G with zero boundary data, any bounded solution must be v(x)=E_x integral_0^tau g(B_t)dt.
proof_idea: The process u(B_t)+integral_0^t g(B_s)ds is a local martingale up to tau; stop and let t approach tau.
exam_use: Use for expected occupation times, expected exit times, and source-term boundary problems.
pitfalls: Sign conventions vary; here the PDE is 1/2 Delta u=-g for a positive occupation formula.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Expected exit time solves Poisson's equation
summary: Taking g=1 gives v(x)=E_x tau, which solves 1/2 Delta v=-1 in G with zero boundary values at regular points.
proof_idea: This is the Poisson representation specialized to unit source.
exam_use: A common prelim computation: guess radial v in a ball and verify by Laplacian and boundary values.
pitfalls: Finite expectation needs domain control; unbounded domains may have infinite exit time.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Poisson solution vanishes at regular boundary points
summary: If G and g are bounded and y is a regular boundary point, then v(x)=E_x integral_0^tau g(B_t)dt tends to zero as x tends to y from inside G.
proof_idea: Regularity makes tau small with high probability; boundedness controls the remaining tail.
exam_use: Use to verify the zero boundary condition for occupation-time solutions.
pitfalls: Regularity and boundedness assumptions are doing real work here.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Half-space Green function by reflection
summary: For the half-space, the killed Green function equals the full-space potential at y minus the full-space potential at the reflected point y_bar.
proof_idea: Use the reflection principle/image method so the difference vanishes on the boundary.
exam_use: Compute occupation times before exiting a half-space.
pitfalls: The reflected term has the same singularity mirrored across the boundary, not at the original y.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Ball Green function by Kelvin transform
summary: For the unit ball in d>=3, G_D(x,y)=G(x,y)-|y|^(2-d)G(x,y/|y|^2); a logarithmic analog holds in d=2.
proof_idea: The Kelvin-reflected singularity makes the boundary value cancel on |x|=1.
exam_use: Use for explicit occupation densities in balls.
pitfalls: The formula has a singular-looking y=0 case that must be handled by a limit or separately.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Schrodinger boundary-value martingale
summary: For 1/2 Delta u+c(x)u=0 in G, u(B_t) exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds) is a local martingale up to the exit time.
proof_idea: Apply Ito's formula and the product rule; the PDE cancels the drift after weighting by the exponential potential.
exam_use: Foundation for boundary Feynman-Kac formulas and exponential exit-time moments.
pitfalls: The sign of c controls integrability; positive c can make the expectation infinite.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Positive potential can destroy bounded representation
summary: For G=(-a,a), c=gamma>0, and boundary value 1, the ODE solution exists only away from eigenvalue obstructions and exponential exit moments can blow up.
proof_idea: Solve 1/2 u''+gamma u=0 with boundary values and inspect zeros of cos(a sqrt(2 gamma)).
exam_use: Use as a warning that Feynman-Kac with positive potential needs integrability assumptions.
pitfalls: Bounded c does not automatically imply E_x exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds) is finite.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Gauge function for exponential lifetime integrability
summary: The gauge w(x)=E_x exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds) measures whether the Schrodinger Feynman-Kac representation is finite.
proof_idea: Local exponential exit-time bounds plus a covering argument propagate finiteness through connected domains.
exam_use: Check this before asserting uniqueness or boundary representations with exponential weights.
pitfalls: Finiteness at one point and boundedness over the domain require separate hypotheses in the chapter.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Small-set exponential exit bound
summary: For each theta>0, sufficiently small open sets H have uniformly bounded E_x exp(theta tau_H).
proof_idea: Use occupation-time/tail bounds for Brownian exit from small-measure sets and sum the exponential series.
exam_use: Technical tool that makes the gauge theorem work.
pitfalls: Small Lebesgue measure is the key condition, not merely small diameter in the statement.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Gauge finiteness propagates on connected domains
summary: If G is connected and the gauge w is not identically infinite, then w(x)<infinity for every x in G; if |G|<infinity, w is bounded.
proof_idea: Local comparison across balls gives openness of the finite set, and connectedness plus small-set control gives closed propagation.
exam_use: Use to justify uniform integrability in Schrodinger uniqueness proofs.
pitfalls: Connectedness matters; separate components need separate checks.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Schrodinger Dirichlet representation
summary: Under bounded connected domain, bounded continuous f and c, and finite gauge, the bounded solution is v(x)=E_x[f(B_tau) exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds)].
proof_idea: Stop the weighted martingale, use boundedness of the gauge for uniform integrability, and pass to the exit time.
exam_use: Use for boundary-value problems with killing or creation potentials.
pitfalls: Without the finite-gauge assumption, the formula may be infinite or uniqueness may fail.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Schrodinger boundary condition at regular points
summary: At regular boundary points, the Schrodinger representation tends to the boundary value f(y).
proof_idea: Split paths by small exit time and nearby exit location, then control the remaining event with bounded gauge.
exam_use: Use to finish verification of boundary data for the weighted representation.
pitfalls: The exponential weight adds a tail-control requirement absent from the plain Dirichlet problem.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Holder potential gives C2 Schrodinger solution
summary: With the chapter's bounded-domain/gauge assumptions and Holder continuous c, the Schrodinger representation is C2 and solves 1/2 Delta v+c v=0.
proof_idea: Reduce locally to Poisson-equation regularity with source c(x)v(x).
exam_use: Use to upgrade the probabilistic representation to a classical solution.
pitfalls: Smoothness is interior; boundary regularity is a separate issue.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 9 PDF
title: Exponential exit moment from an interval
summary: For tau exiting (-a,a), E_x exp(gamma tau)=cos(x sqrt(2 gamma))/cos(a sqrt(2 gamma)) when 0<gamma<pi^2/(8a^2), and is infinite at or above the threshold.
proof_idea: Solve the ODE 1/2 u''+gamma u=0 with boundary value one and use the gauge/uniqueness result below the first eigenvalue.
exam_use: A high-yield explicit computation connecting Brownian exit times with eigenvalues.
pitfalls: The threshold is the first Dirichlet eigenvalue for the interval; at the threshold the denominator vanishes.
